title: Functional brain imaging and population-level visits to urban spaces

data_scope.participants: small US sample who had never visited Lisbon (exact n in Methods/Supplementary, not stated in abstract)
data_scope.target_variable: cell-level photograph density around Lisbon as visit proxy

core_conclusion: Brain activity in a small lab sample can forecast population behavior in a city participants never visited: when US adults viewed 160 geotagged Flickr photos of Lisbon during fMRI, ventromedial prefrontal cortex (reward-valuation) activity predicted the real density of photographs around those locations, a proxy for population-level visits. Reward-related neural responses to the built environment help shape where people go, offering a brain-based signal for designing attractive, sustainable cities.
why_it_matters: Extends neuroforecasting from media/markets into the spatial-urban domain; proposes vmPFC reward valuation as a mechanism for place attractiveness; validates geotagged social-media photo density as a cheap visitation proxy against the brain; gives planners a pre-construction screening signal for whether a space will be used.
why_nature: Genuinely interdisciplinary (neuroscience + social-media geodata + urban science); surprising, generalizable claim (few brains predict a city's population behavior); couples a neural mechanism to a real policy outcome; agenda-setting for the emerging neurourbanism sub-discipline.
added_value_to_my_research: Neural seed labels calibrating a global photo-density / visitation foundation model via multi-source fusion
vmPFC attractiveness as a destination-appeal term in learned gravity / R2SFCA supply weighting
Explainable ML over street-view features to attribute reward-driven attractiveness to plannable design variables
Attractiveness layer as a covariate in mental-health accessibility and restorative-space siting
Physics-informed graph operator mapping appeal field + network + population to transferable visitation surfaces
